title: Northern Farmer (New Style)
content:
I.
DOSNï¿½T thou ï¿½ear my ï¿½erseï¿½s legs, as they canters awaï¿½y?
Proputty, proputty, proputtyï¿½thatï¿½s what I ï¿½ears ï¿½em saï¿½y.
Proputty, proputty, proputtyï¿½Sam, thouï¿½s an ass for thy paaï¿½ns:
Theerï¿½s moor sense iï¿½ one oï¿½ ï¿½is legs nor in all thy braï¿½ins.

II.
Woï¿½theerï¿½s a craw to pluck wiï¿½ tha, Sam: yonï¿½s parsonï¿½s ï¿½ouseï¿½
Dosnï¿½t thou knaw that a man mun be eï¿½ther a man or a mouse?
Time to think on it then; for thouï¿½ll be twenty to weeï¿½k.
Proputty, proputtyï¿½woï¿½ then, woï¿½let ma ï¿½ear mysï¿½n speï¿½k.

III.
Me anï¿½ thy muther, Sammy, ï¿½as beï¿½n a-talkinï¿½ oï¿½ thee;
Thouï¿½s beï¿½n talkinï¿½ to muther, anï¿½ she beï¿½n a tellinï¿½ it me.
Thouï¿½ll not marry for munnyï¿½thouï¿½s sweet upoï¿½ parsonï¿½s lassï¿½
Noï¿½thouï¿½ll marry fur luvvï¿½anï¿½ we boï¿½th on us thinks tha an ass.

IV.
Seeï¿½d her todaï¿½y goï¿½ byï¿½Saï¿½intï¿½s-daï¿½yï¿½they was ringing the bells.
Sheï¿½s a beauty thou thinksï¿½anï¿½ soï¿½ is scoors oï¿½ gells,
Them as ï¿½as munny anï¿½ allï¿½wotï¿½s a beauty?ï¿½the flower as blaws.
But proputty, proputty sticks, anï¿½ proputty, proputty graws.

V.
Doï¿½ant be stunt: taï¿½k time: I knaws what maï¿½kes tha sa mad.
Warnï¿½t I craï¿½zed fur the lasses mysï¿½n when I wur a lad?
But I knawed a Quaï¿½ker feller as often ï¿½as towd ma this:
ï¿½Doï¿½nt thou marry for munny, but goï¿½ wheer munny is!ï¿½

VI.
Anï¿½ I went wheer munny war: anï¿½ thy muther coom to ï¿½and,
Wiï¿½ lots oï¿½ munny laaï¿½d by, anï¿½ a nicetish bit oï¿½ land.
Maï¿½ybe she warnï¿½t a beauty:ï¿½I niver giv it a thowtï¿½
But warnï¿½t she as good to cuddle anï¿½ kiss as a lass as ï¿½ant nowt?

VII.
Parsonï¿½s lass ï¿½ant nowt, anï¿½ she weï¿½nt ï¿½a nowt when ï¿½eï¿½s deï¿½d,
Mun be a guvness, lad, or summut, and addle her breï¿½d:
Why? fur ï¿½eï¿½s nobbut a curate, anï¿½ weï¿½nt niver git hissen clear,
Anï¿½ ï¿½e maï¿½de the bed as ï¿½e ligs on afoor ï¿½e coomed to the shere.

VIII.
An thin ï¿½e coomï¿½d to the parish wiï¿½ lots oï¿½ Varsity debt,
Stook to his taaï¿½l they did, anï¿½ ï¿½e ï¿½ant got shut on ï¿½em yet.
Anï¿½ ï¿½e ligs on ï¿½is back iï¿½ the grip, wiï¿½ noï¿½n to lend ï¿½im a shovv,
Woorse nor a far-welterï¿½d yowe: fur, Sammy, ï¿½e married fur luvv.

IX.
Luvv? whatï¿½s luvv? thou can luvv thy lass anï¿½ ï¿½er munny too,
Maï¿½kinï¿½ ï¿½em goaï¿½ togither as theyï¿½ve good right to do.
Couldï¿½n I luvv thy muther by cause oï¿½ ï¿½er munny laaï¿½d by?
Naï¿½yï¿½fur I luvvï¿½d ï¿½er a vast sight moor fur it: reï¿½son why.

X.
Ay anï¿½ thy muther says thou wants to marry the lass,
Cooms of a gentleman burn: anï¿½ we boï¿½th on us thinks tha an ass.
Woï¿½ then, proputty, wiltha?ï¿½an ass as near as mays nowtï¿½
Woï¿½ then, wiltha? dangtha!ï¿½the bees is as fell as owt.

XI.
Breï¿½k me a bit oï¿½ the esh for his ï¿½eï¿½d, lad, out oï¿½ the fence!
Gentleman burn! whatï¿½s gentleman burn? is it shillins anï¿½ pence?
Proputty, proputtyï¿½s ivrything ï¿½ere, anï¿½, Sammy, Iï¿½m blest
If it isnï¿½t the saï¿½me oop yonder, fur them as ï¿½as itï¿½s the best.

XII.
Tisï¿½n them as ï¿½as munny as breaks into ï¿½ouses anï¿½ steï¿½ls,
Them as ï¿½as coï¿½ts to their backs anï¿½ taï¿½kes their regular meï¿½ls.
Noï¿½, but itï¿½s them as niver knaws wheer a meï¿½lï¿½s to be ï¿½ad.
Taï¿½ke my word for it, Sammy, the poor in a loomp is bad.

XIII.
Them or thir feythers, tha sees, mun ï¿½a beï¿½n a laï¿½zy lot,
Fur work mun ï¿½a gone to the gittinï¿½ whiniver munny was got.
Feyther ï¿½ad ammost nowt; leï¿½stways ï¿½is munny was ï¿½id.
But ï¿½e tued anï¿½ moilï¿½d ï¿½issï¿½n deï¿½d, an ï¿½e died a good un, ï¿½e did.

XIV.
Loook thou theer wheer Wrigglesby beck cooms out by the ï¿½ill!
Feyther run oop to the farm, anï¿½ I runs oop to the mill;
Anï¿½ Iï¿½ll run oop to the brig, anï¿½ that thouï¿½ll live to see;
And if thou marries a good un Iï¿½ll leï¿½ve the land to thee.

XV.
Thimï¿½s my noï¿½tions, Sammy, wheerby I means to stick;
But if thou marries a bad un, Iï¿½ll leï¿½ve the land to Dick.ï¿½
Coom oop, proputty, proputtyï¿½thatï¿½s what I ï¿½ears ï¿½im saï¿½yï¿½
Proputty, proputty, proputtyï¿½canter anï¿½ canter awaï¿½y.
meaning:
About: Alfred Tennyson, 1st Baron Tennyson (6 August 1809 ï¿½ 6 October 1892) was Poet Laureate of the United Kingdom and is one of the most popular English poets.

Much of his verse was based on classical mythological themes, although In Memoriam was written to commemorate his best friend Arthur Hallam, a fellow poet and classmate at Trinity College, Cambridge who was engaged to Tennyson's sister but died from a cerebral hemorrhage. One of Tennyson's most famous works is Idylls of the King (1885), a series of narrative poems based entirely on King Arthur and the Arthurian tales, as thematically suggested by Sir Thomas Malory's earlier tales on the legendary king. The work was dedicated to Prince Albert, the husband of Queen Victoria. During his career, Lord Tennyson attempted drama, but his plays enjoyed little success even in his lifetime.

Tennyson wrote a number of phrases that have become commonplaces of the English language, including: "nature, red in tooth and claw", "better to have loved and lost", "Theirs not to reason why, Theirs but to do and die", and "My strength is as the strength of ten, Because my heart is pure".

He is the second most frequently quoted writer in the English language, after Shakespeare.
